Airline Route Profitability

DATA 2401

Niyog Uprety & Shaheer Ahmed

April 2026

About the Dataset

  • Source: Kaggle, Airline Route Profitability Dataset by Muhammad Waleed
  • Size: 7,974 flights across multiple routes, seasons, and aircraft types
  • Goal: Figure out what actually drives airline profitability. Is it the route? The season? How full the plane is?
  • Key metric: We use both Profit and Profit Margin, because raw profit alone can be misleading

Data Cleaning

  • Checked for missing values across all 33 columns
  • Replaced missing numbers with the median of that column
  • Replaced missing text values with “Unknown”
  • Dataset was fairly clean with no major issues found
## Missing values after cleaning: 0

How Does Profit Spread Across Flights?

Most flights earn between $0 and $500K. Some lose money. A small number of high-performing flights earn significantly more, with the bulk of the data reaching close to $700K.

Are Long-Haul Routes More Profitable?

Long-haul earns more dollars, but the margin gap shrinks when we divide by revenue. Bigger scale does not necessarily mean more efficient.

Does Season Affect Profit?

Peak season is more profitable on average, but the boxes overlap a lot. Season plays a role, but it is not the whole story.

Season definitions: Peak = June to August and late December (holidays and summer vacation). Shoulder = April to May and September to October (moderate travel). Normal = March and November. Low = January to February (slowest travel period).

Demand Level is the Strongest Driver

This is the clearest pattern in the dataset. High demand flights are more profitable in both metrics, with much less overlap between groups than we saw with season.

Fuller Flights = More Profit

Each dot is one flight. The red line is the break-even point at zero profit. All flights in this dataset already have a high load factor, so the data looks compact and clustered. Even then, a lot of flights still fall below the break-even line. That tells us load factor alone is not enough. Other costs like fuel, crew, and route length also determine whether a flight makes money.

Profit Over Time

Average profit is actually highest in January and December, and lowest in June and July. In peak summer months, airlines run far more total flights, including many lower-demand ones that bring the average down. No month averages below zero.

Key Takeaways

  • Demand level is the strongest predictor of profitability, stronger than season
  • Long-haul routes earn more dollars, but are not necessarily more efficient per dollar of revenue
  • Load factor is critical. Flights with low seat occupancy are the most likely to lose money
  • The monthly trend is counterintuitive. Average profit is highest in winter and lowest in summer
  • Best combination: Peak season + Long Haul consistently produces the highest profit and margin
